nativeRange.habitat: Forested tributaries and blackwater streams with tannin-stained, soft and acidic water; typically found in areas with dense riparian vegetation.

decorPreferences: open swimming space in the foreground

diet.notes: Readily accepts small live and frozen foods such as bloodworm, Daphnia and brine shrimp, as well as dried flakes, micropellets and granules. A varied diet tends to bring out the best coloration.

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by h080; 3888x2592. Sharp, high-res single specimen in a planted tank with vivid orange-red lateral stripe and red eye, whole fish in frame - best overall.
Photo by Gonzalo Valenzuela; 1024x768. Crisp lateral profile, brilliant continuous red stripe and red eye against green plants.
Photo by Brian Gratwicke; 1024x683. Sharp and vibrant, shows two individuals - better represents this schooling species; slightly darker body tone.

summary: A hardy, peaceful schooling tetra from Guyanese blackwater streams, recognised by its vivid orange-red horizontal stripe. Suitable for most community setups and generally considered an excellent beginner fish.
careNotes:
Best kept in groups of 6 or more — smaller groups tend to be shy and stressed. The species generally does well in most community tanks, though it tends to look its most vibrant in planted aquariums with a dark substrate and some tannin staining. While adaptable, it is commonly considered to show better coloration in soft, slightly acidic water. An albino captive-bred form is occasionally available in the hobby.

Not to be confused with the Glowlight Rasbora (Trigonostigma hengeli), which is a completely different family (Cyprinidae), or with GloFish — the latter are genetically modified Zebra Danios (Danio rerio) with no relation to this species.

Note on taxonomy: the Glowlight Tetra has traditionally been classified in the family Characidae, and that placement remains the most widely used in the aquarium hobby. Some 2024 phylogenomic revisions have proposed reassigning the species to Acestrorhamphidae, but most aquarium hobby sources continue to use the Characidae classification.
breedingNotes: Breeding has been reported as moderately achievable in captivity with a dedicated breeding setup. A separate, heavily planted or mesh-bottomed tank with soft, acidic, warm water (around 26-28°C) is generally recommended. Spawning is egg-scattering; adults have been known to eat eggs and fry, so separating adults after spawning is advisable. Typical broods of 100-150 fry have been documented, with eggs hatching within approximately 24-36 hours. Fry are small and generally require infusoria or very fine foods initially.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked size, temperature, pH, hardness, and native range. FishBase lists max TL as 3.3 cm; aquarium hobby sources consistently report up to 4 cm. Temperature and hardness broadly consistent. pH range slightly wider on FishBase (6.0-8.0) vs SeriouslyFish (5.5-7.5); used SeriouslyFish range as it is more aligned with the species' natural blackwater habitat and aquarium hobby consensus.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (3.8 cm), temperature (23-28°C), hardness (6-15 dGH), lifespan (2-4 years), and grouping minimum. Minimum tank volume listed as 38L (10 gal) — considered too low for a school of 6+; SeriouslyFish 70L and brief guidance of 75L used instead.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op
additional.notes: Confirmed size (~4 cm), community suitability, and natural habitat description (tannin-filled South American waters). No detailed parameter data available on this page; used as supporting context only.

fish.breedingNotes: The Glowlight Tetra is an egg-scattering spawner that does best in soft, acidic, warm water for breeding. Adults will readily consume their own eggs, so removing the parents from the breeding tank promptly after spawning is advisable. Newly hatched fry are very small and generally require infusoria or similarly fine foods in their first days.
